title: BertNet: Harvesting Knowledge Graphs from Pretrained Language Models

structured.summary: This paper introduces BertNet, a novel framework to automatically harvest large-scale, explicit knowledge graphs with arbitrary relations from pretrained language models using minimal input prompts and example entity pairs. It proposes automatic prompt generation and weighting combined with an efficient entity pair search to extract symbolic knowledge, enabling interpretable analysis of LM knowledge without fine-tuning or external training data.

strengths.point: Novel framework enabling extraction of arbitrary new relations from any pretrained LM
strengths.evidence: The method requires only a minimal relation definition (a prompt and few examples) and works across various LMs (BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT, GPT-3) without fine-tuning.

strengths.point: Effective automatic prompt creation and weighting improves knowledge extraction accuracy
strengths.evidence: Automatic prompt paraphrasing combined with a scoring function for prompt weighting yields higher accuracy and better precision-recall tradeoffs than human-written or single prompts.

strengths.point: Efficient entity pair search algorithm enables scalable harvesting of large KGs
strengths.evidence: The proposed pruning and re-ranking strategy allows searching large entity sets to extract hundreds of thousands of tuples across hundreds of relations.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical validation on diverse relations and LMs
strengths.evidence: Experiments on ConceptNet, LAMA, human-written, and auto-generated relations show accuracy up to 88%, discovery of novel knowledge beyond existing KGs, and insights into LM knowledge capacity related to model size and pretraining.

strengths.point: Provides interpretable symbolic KG representations of LM knowledge
strengths.evidence: The extracted tuples serve as explicit, navigable knowledge graphs, facilitating analysis and understanding of black-box pretrained LMs.

weaknesses.point: Limited comparison to recent prompt engineering and tuning methods
weaknesses.evidence: The paper compares mainly to AutoPrompt and LPAQA but omits other recent prompt tuning or knowledge probing methods, weakening novelty claims.

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablation studies isolating prompt filtering and weighting effects
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.1 introduces both filtering and weighting but only combined improvements are reported, leaving unclear the contribution of each component.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient details on paraphrase model and filtering thresholds hinder reproducibility
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not specify which off-the-shelf paraphrase model was used nor the exact criteria or thresholds for prompt filtering.

weaknesses.point: Entity pair search algorithm lacks detailed implementation and scalability analysis
weaknesses.evidence: Key parameters such as pruning thresholds, candidate set sizes, and computational complexity are not described, making replication difficult.

weaknesses.point: Human annotation protocol lacks inter-annotator agreement and disagreement resolution details
weaknesses.evidence: Without reporting agreement statistics or adjudication methods, the reliability of accuracy metrics is uncertain.

weaknesses.point: Comparisons to baselines are not fully fair due to differences in training data usage
weaknesses.evidence: Baselines like COMET and text mining methods use external corpora or training data, whereas BertNet relies solely on pretrained LMs without fine-tuning.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation and analysis of n-ary and complex relations
weaknesses.evidence: Although the method claims to handle n-ary relations, evaluation is sparse and lacks detailed comparison to baselines.

weaknesses.point: No discussion on handling noisy, contradictory, or biased knowledge extraction
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not address how conflicting tuples are detected or resolved, nor does it propose mitigation strategies for biases inherent in LMs.

weaknesses.point: Ethical considerations are briefly mentioned but lack depth and concrete mitigation
weaknesses.evidence: Potential risks of harmful or biased knowledge extraction are acknowledged but not elaborated with safeguards or solutions.

weaknesses.point: Terminology inconsistencies and unclear explanations reduce clarity
weaknesses.evidence: Terms like 'consistency score' and 'compatibility score' are used interchangeably without clear definitions; scoring function intuition is not well explained.

weaknesses.point: No analysis of prompt length or complexity impact on extraction quality
weaknesses.evidence: The effect of initial prompt linguistic properties on paraphrase generation and final KG quality is not studied.

weaknesses.point: Generalisability limited to English and generic domains
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus on English LMs and generic knowledge relations without evaluation on other languages or specialized domains.

weaknesses.point: Precision-recall results lack statistical significance testing
weaknesses.evidence: No confidence intervals or significance analyses are reported for precision-recall curves, leaving robustness of improvements unclear.

questions.question: How sensitive is the method to the choice and completeness of the entity set used for candidate generation?
questions.motivation: Since entity pair search depends on a predefined entity set, missing important entities could limit knowledge extraction coverage.

questions.question: Can the framework be extended or adapted to domain-specific LMs and specialized knowledge graphs?
questions.motivation: Evaluating applicability beyond generic English LMs is important for practical use in specialized fields like healthcare or scientific domains.

questions.question: What mechanisms can be introduced to detect and resolve contradictory or noisy knowledge tuples extracted from LMs?
questions.motivation: Ensuring the quality and consistency of the harvested KG is critical for downstream applications and trustworthiness.

questions.question: How does the linguistic complexity or length of initial prompts affect the quality and diversity of generated paraphrases and final KG extraction?
questions.motivation: Understanding prompt design impact can guide users to provide better inputs and improve extraction performance.

questions.question: What are the ethical risks associated with extracting potentially biased or harmful knowledge from LMs, and how can these be mitigated?
questions.motivation: Given the societal impact of deploying knowledge graphs, concrete strategies for bias detection and mitigation are necessary.

critique_points.point_001: Limited comparison to recent prompt engineering and tuning methods. The paper compares mainly to AutoPrompt and LPAQA but omits other recent prompt tuning or knowledge probing methods, weakening novelty claims
critique_points.point_002: Lack of ablation studies isolating prompt filtering and weighting effects. Section 3.1 introduces both filtering and weighting but only combined improvements are reported, leaving unclear the contribution of each component
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient details on paraphrase model and filtering thresholds hinder reproducibility. The paper does not specify which off-the-shelf paraphrase model was used nor the exact criteria or thresholds for prompt filtering
critique_points.point_004: Entity pair search algorithm lacks detailed implementation and scalability analysis. Key parameters such as pruning thresholds, candidate set sizes, and computational complexity are not described, making replication difficult
critique_points.point_005: Human annotation protocol lacks inter-annotator agreement and disagreement resolution details. Without reporting agreement statistics or adjudication methods, the reliability of accuracy metrics is uncertain
critique_points.point_006: Comparisons to baselines are not fully fair due to differences in training data usage. Baselines like COMET and text mining methods use external corpora or training data, whereas BertNet relies solely on pretrained LMs without fine-tuning
critique_points.point_007: Limited evaluation and analysis of n-ary and complex relations. Although the method claims to handle n-ary relations, evaluation is sparse and lacks detailed comparison to baselines
critique_points.point_008: No discussion on handling noisy, contradictory, or biased knowledge extraction. The paper does not address how conflicting tuples are detected or resolved, nor does it propose mitigation strategies for biases inherent in LMs
critique_points.point_009: Ethical considerations are briefly mentioned but lack depth and concrete mitigation. Potential risks of harmful or biased knowledge extraction are acknowledged but not elaborated with safeguards or solutions
critique_points.point_010: Terminology inconsistencies and unclear explanations reduce clarity. Terms like 'consistency score' and 'compatibility score' are used interchangeably without clear definitions; scoring function intuition is not well explained
critique_points.point_011: No analysis of prompt length or complexity impact on extraction quality. The effect of initial prompt linguistic properties on paraphrase generation and final KG quality is not studied
critique_points.point_012: Generalisability limited to English and generic domains. Experiments focus on English LMs and generic knowledge relations without evaluation on other languages or specialized domains
critique_points.point_013: Precision-recall results lack statistical significance testing. No confidence intervals or significance analyses are reported for precision-recall curves, leaving robustness of improvements unclear
